title: The Macro Minute: Should investors de-risk ahead of Kevin Warsh’s Senate confirmation hearing?

Happy Monday out there team 42. It's
your skipper here Darius Dale to present
our macro minute for Monday, April 20th,
2026. As always, we'll start with the
executive summary from today's lead off
morning note. So, let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, should
investors de-risk ahead of Kevin Warsh's
Senate confirmation hearing tomorrow?
Our answer is, as we highlighted late
last week, there are plenty of reasons
for high turnover investors to
anticipate a near-term pullback in risk
assets according to various elements of
our risk management toolkit. Tomorrow's
Senate confirmation hearing represents
Fed chair nominee Kevin Warsh's first
public commentary on interest rate since
his nomination and may spur interest
rate volatility. Curve steepeners are
the obvious trade here as any signal
from Warsh favoring monetary easing in
2026 and tolerance for conflict-induced
inflation could catalyze gains in short
rates while simultaneously causing a
sell-off in the long bond. So, yeah,
continue reviewing for our deeper
thoughts in terms of Kevin Warsh's
likely impact on the monetary policy
cycle and the liquidity cycle
specifically as it relates to
potentially swapping
cash bank reserves for Treasuries in
terms of banks high-quality liquid asset
exposure which would ultimately allow a
more smooth and durable reduction of the
Fed's balance sheet which is one of the
key, if not the key, question in all of
global financial markets outside of the
closure of the Strait of Hormuz. So,
transitioning to our 42 macro dashboard
here, as always, we'll wrap up with a
question from our community. This one's
titled VT versus SPYM with higher energy
prices. It says, "A narrative that is
floating around this market in the
context of the Iran conflict is that the
USA is less sensitive to oil and energy
prices because of the shale revolution
and USA energy independency. In other
words, this is everyone's excuse to buy
S&P 500 and Nasdaq. Meanwhile, other
countries or regions like the EU or
parts of Asia are importing or importing
oil and energy. If there are other parts
of the world ex-USA are truly more
sensitive to increasing energy prices,
is 42 macro still the view that VT will
outperform SPYM in this next bull run
higher?" So, the first thing I'll say is
our view that VT
is, which is the total global equity
market, is likely to outperform the US
equity market over the long term is not
at all influenced by the Strait of
Hormuz dynamics. Recall that there are
two fundamental research pillars that
are underpinning our expectation for the
total global equity market to continue
to outperform the US-only equity market.
Number one, we believe that there's
going to be a convergence in the
productivity, profitability, and
ultimately valuations for international
companies and stocks relative to the US
counterparts as AI gets diffused
throughout the global economy and then
we also believe that the US's net
international investment deficit will
narrow over time as the world transit
continues to transition to a multipolar
world and as we continue to see the US
current account deficit narrow, likely
narrow,
primarily as a function of some of the
trade policy dynamics that we've seen
out of the Trump administration. So, in
our view, this is going to be a
long-term, you know, 5 to 10 year
trade notwithstanding the fact that if
we continue to see the nascent energy
supply shock continue to persist or even
devolve, then it's highly likely that US
equities will outperform the
international equity counterparts over
the medium term, but again, that's a
medium-term perspective. From a
longer-term perspective, we think, you
know, the horse has left the barn from
the perspective of the total
international equity market
outperforming the US equity market. So,
we'll wrap it up there. Darius Dale here
presenting our macro minute for Monday,
April 20th, 2026. Best of luck out here
today. We'll catch back here tomorrow.
Cheers.
